name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Cómo eras antes?

sideB.text: What were you like before?
How were you before?
What did you use to be like?

cards.note:
**[er:86]**

[What... like?] refers to past descriptions of character or appearance, whereas [How...?] refers to a past state or condition.

cards.sideA: Lo que importa es que sea entendible.

sideB.text: What matters is that it's understandable.
The important thing is that it's clear.
All that matters is that it makes sense.
What matters is it's understandable.

cards.note:
~What matters it's to be understandable~ is incorrect because it contains a redundant [it's]. The phrase [What matters] acts as the subject, so you should connect it directly to the verb [is], as in [What matters is that it's understandable].

~What matters is to be understandable~ is unnatural. You need to specify what is being understandable. It is better to use a full clause like [is that it's understandable] or specify the object like [for it to be understandable].

cards.sideA: Ocurrio una desincronizacion en el procedimiento.

sideB.text: A desynchronization occurred in the procedure.
A desync occurred in the procedure.
An out-of-sync issue occurred during the procedure.

cards.note: ~It happened a desynchronization in the procedure.~ and ~It occurred a desynchronization in the procedure.~ are incorrect. In English, you cannot use [It] as a dummy subject to introduce an event in this way. You must either make the event the subject itself, like [A desynchronization occurred], or use [There was a desynchronization].

cards.sideA: Este producto sobra.

sideB.text: This product is extra.
This product is left over.
We have this product left over.

cards.note:
**[pv:203]**

~This product is left~ is not entirely accurate for 'sobra'. [Left] means remaining ('queda'), while [left over] specifically means extra or surplus ('sobra').

cards.sideA: Está pendiente eso para mañana.

sideB.text: That's pending for tomorrow.
That is pending for tomorrow.
That remains pending for tomorrow.
We left that for tomorrow.
That's left for tomorrow.

cards.note: ~That's left over for tomorrow~ is incorrect because [left over] means extra or surplus, not pending. Use [pending] or [left].

cards.sideA: Aún no he tenido respuesta de él.

sideB.text: I haven't heard back from him yet.
I still haven't received a reply from him.
I haven't had a response from him yet.
I haven't had an answer from him yet.
I haven't heard anything from him yet.

cards.sideA: Aún no he sabido nada acerca de eso.

sideB.text: I haven't heard anything about that yet.
I still haven't received any news regarding that.
I haven't found out anything about that yet.

cards.note:
**[pv:59]**

~I didn't know anything about that yet~ and ~I haven't known anything about that yet~ are incorrect. Although 'know' translates to 'saber', in English [know] is a state verb (the condition of having knowledge), not an action verb. In Spanish, 'saber' can mean both the state ('yo sé') and the action of receiving news ('no he sabido nada'). In English, when you mean 'enterarse' or receiving an update, you must use action verbs like [hear] or [find out]. Therefore, you say 'I haven't heard' (no he tenido noticias) instead of 'I haven't known'. Additionally, [yet] typically pairs with the present perfect.

cards.sideA: Aquí pasamos los números a formato texto.

sideB.text: Here we convert the numbers to text format.
Here we pass the numbers into text format.
In this step, we convert the numbers into text format.
Here we transform the numbers to text format.

cards.note: ~Here we parse the numbers to text format.~ is not completely accurate. In programming, [parse] usually means analyzing a text string to extract data or convert it into a different type (like string to integer). To change a number into a text string, the correct terms are [convert], [cast], or [format].

cards.sideA: Para acelerar el proceso, ustedes deberian llamar directamente a Apple.

sideB.text: To speed up the process, you should call Apple directly.
To expedite the process, you should call Apple directly.
In order to speed up the process, you guys should call Apple directly.

cards.sideA: Ellos te pediran tu carnet de identidad para validar tu identidad.

sideB.text: They will ask for your ID card to validate your identity.
They'll ask for your ID to verify your identity.
They will request your ID card to confirm your identity.

cards.sideA: Solicite hablar con alguien que hablara en español.

sideB.text: I requested to speak with someone who speaks Spanish.
I asked to speak to someone who could speak Spanish.
I asked to talk to a Spanish speaker.

cards.sideA: Production stopper, Production blocker y Showstopper significan prácticamente lo mismo.

sideB.text: Production stopper, Production blocker, and Showstopper mean essentially the same thing.
Production stopper, Production blocker, and Showstopper mean practically the same thing.
Production stopper, Production blocker, and Showstopper are basically the same thing.

cards.sideA: Production blocker y Showstopper son los más comunes de usar.

sideB.text: Production blocker and Showstopper are the most commonly used terms.
Production blocker and Showstopper are the most common ones to use.
Production blocker and Showstopper are used the most.

cards.sideA: Production stopper se entiende, pero se usan más Showstopper o Production blocker.

sideB.text: Production stopper is understood, but Showstopper and Production blocker are used more often.
Production stopper is understood, but Showstopper or Production blocker are more commonly used.
People understand Production stopper, but Showstopper and Production blocker are used more.

cards.sideA: Así podemos comenzar con el pie derecho.

sideB.text: That way we can start off on the right foot.
This way we can get off on the right foot.
So we can start on the right foot.

cards.sideA: Prefiero una entrevista de uno a uno (2 personas).

sideB.text: I prefer a one-on-one interview.
I would rather have a one-on-one interview.
I'd prefer a one-to-one interview.

cards.sideA: Testiando los casos uno a uno (un caso a la vez).

sideB.text: Testing the cases one by one.
Testing the cases one at a time.
Testing each case one by one.

cards.sideA: Lo testie sobre la marcha y funcionó.

sideB.text: I tested it on the fly and it worked.
I tested it as I went and it worked.
I tested it on the go and it worked.

cards.sideA: Sobre la marcha iré documentando lo realizado.

sideB.text: I will document what's been done on the fly.
I'll document what is done as we go.
I'll document my progress as I go.

cards.sideA: No ha sido implementado aun esa funcion.

sideB.text: That feature hasn't been implemented yet.
That function has not been implemented yet.
That feature is not implemented yet.

cards.sideA: Esa funcion no ha sido implementada aun.

sideB.text: That feature hasn't been implemented yet.
That function hasn't been implemented yet.
That feature has not yet been implemented.

cards.sideA: El que mas me preocupa es el issue 34.

sideB.text: The one I'm most worried about is issue 34.
The one that concerns me the most is issue 34.
What worries me the most is issue 34.

cards.sideA: No lo he subido aún.

sideB.text: I haven't uploaded it yet.
I haven't pushed it yet.
I still haven't uploaded it.

cards.sideA: Debo rellenar el listado día por medio.

sideB.text: I have to fill out the list every other day.
I need to fill in the list every other day.
I must fill out the list every two days.

cards.sideA: El día de antes de ayer ocurrió.

sideB.text: It happened the day before yesterday.
It occurred the day before yesterday.
That happened the day before yesterday.

cards.sideA: ¿Puedes invitarme a la reunión, por favor?

sideB.text: Can you please invite me to the meeting?
Could you invite me to the meeting, please?
Can you send me an invite to the meeting, please?

cards.sideA: No tengo la invitación a la reunión.

sideB.text: I don't have the invite for the meeting.
I don't have the invitation for the meeting.
I haven't received the meeting invite.

cards.sideA: Que hay acerca de la reunión de hoy.

sideB.text: What about today's meeting?
What's happening with today's meeting?
How about the meeting today?

cards.sideA: ¿Vamos a tener la reunión para hoy en la noche?

sideB.text: Are we going to have the meeting tonight?
Are we still having the meeting tonight?
Will we have the meeting tonight?

cards.sideA: Ok entiendo. Estaré atento.

sideB.text: Ok, I understand. I'll keep an eye out.
Ok, got it. I'll stay tuned.
Ok, I see. I will be on the lookout.
